does: This paper identifies and characterizes three forms of parameter degeneracy in neural networks: linear dependence between activations within layers, linear dependence between backpropagated gradients, and ReLUs that activate identically across datapoints. The authors leverage singular learning theory to argue that degeneracy is inherent to well-generalizing neural networks and develop metrics to detect degenerate structure. They propose that representing networks in ways invariant to degeneracy-exploiting reparameterizations could reveal the underlying algorithmic structure, and present a heuristic argument that modular networks exhibit greater degeneracy, introducing a degeneracy-based metric for identifying such modules.
connects: This work builds on singular learning theory, which establishes that neural networks with more degenerate parameterizations generalize better—connecting statistical properties of learned models to their internal structure. It extends mechanistic interpretability efforts that seek to reverse-engineer learned computations by decomposing networks into interpretable components. The paper responds to the core challenge that parameter redundancy obscures interpretable structure, building on prior work identifying superfluous weights and modular organization in neural networks. It relates to research on neural network reparameterization invariances and the connection between model capacity, generalization, and parameter geometry.
enables: This framework could enable improved mechanistic interpretability tools by filtering out non-computational parameters, making manual and automated circuit analysis more tractable. It suggests a principled approach to extracting modular structure from trained networks by leveraging degeneracy patterns. The work points toward developing reparameterization-invariant representations of neural networks that expose algorithmic content while removing superficial parameterization choices. Future work could apply these degeneracy metrics to larger models, develop automated methods for exploiting invariances to simplify networks, and establish formal connections between degeneracy structure and interpretable circuits, potentially enabling better understanding of how learned algorithms emerge during training.
